Great Crested Newt Hibernacula and scrub planting site.  Hibernacula consists of Logs, stone, soil, terram, mixed native scrub.  Laminated notice immediately adjacent to hibernacula.  Works are required under the Natural England development licence for works associated with electrical substation works.  Hibernacula is being created as compensation for loss of 0.6ha of potential Great Crested Newt habitat.  Structure is being placed on flat ground or ground sloping away from the track and at a distance of approximately 47m from the line.  The structure will be constructed using stakes and sleepers to avoid movement in any direction.  Scrub will be planted in combination with stakes and spiral stem protectors.  Structure is being built inside the railway boundary fence.  Location of hibernacula has been designed to be sufficiently far from the running rails, access points and roads to avoid or at least make it extremely unlikely to be an issue to future works.

The Little Somerford Access Point is surrounded by a Great Crested Newt habitat with confirmed presence of a high population. As a result, the Little Someford RRAP shall not be used without adequate, robust, and strict control measures in place. These measures should be documented <e.g., within an Ecology Management Plan> and briefed out through the Work Package Plan and Task Briefing Sheet process as well as Toolbox Talks and clearly visible on site. The controls will depend on the activities being carried out and should include, but not be restricted to: Material Storage above ground <e.g., on pallets> to avoid GCN using it as resting place. Heras fence to exclude plant and vehicles from affecting the GCN habitat. Ecological walkover prior to the start of the work activities. Ecological supervision of work activities. Regular site inspections by an ecologist. Ecological awareness training. If any more information is required, please contact Kim Olliver, Network Rail Ecologist.

Access gates are 1.8m height with a width plain galvenised palisade gate provided at the bottom of the access point. Allows access to the area to the rear of Barnwood Jn between the SWM2 and CHL. The 2.5m wide gates open towards the railway and are secured with a BR No.2 padlock <aka cliplock>, there is a new access point sign.~
~
There is ample parking at this location adjacent to the access point on a tarmac access track and within the access point which has had MoT type 1 rolled and compacted to form a level surface. The minimum width of the access track is 5m, to note the slope to track is steeper than would normally be expected, however works have been completed to reduce the height of the ramp at the top to minimise the risk of grounding. However, care should still be taken to prevent grounding of vehicles - i.e. speed not to be excessive.

Substation compound houses outdoor switchgear <25kv> for 2 circuits - SGT9B & SGT2B; each circuit has 2x Cable Sealing Ends, 1x Disconnector+Earth Switch, 1x HAM Unit.   There is 1x Portable Relay Room <PRR> which houses protection and Control Equipment. Voltage - 25KV. Owner/Maintainer National Grid PLC. Each circuit has: 6 x 180mm diameter ducts for Power cable <1000sq.mm conductor, copper, 64mm dia>  1x110mm duct for Fibre Optic, 2x100mm Duct for broken neutral cable. Geological importance for its exposure of a section through the Bath Oolite and Upper Rags/Forest Marble.  Of prime importance at this locality are the coral "reef-knolls" developed in the Corsham Coral Bed at the base of the Upper Rags, and the site is the type-locality of this bed.  The reefs are both of sedimentological and palaeontological interest.  Obtain specialist environmental advise before undertaking ground works or vegetation clearance.

Box Tunnel is used throughout its length by roosting bats particularly Pipistrelle spp and Myotis spp.  These bats roost in weep holes and general crevices throughout the length of the tunnel.  The tunnel is used for both summer roosting and winter hibernation.  No works that directly impact a weep hole or crevice ie drilling, or filling in of weep holes or crevices should be undertaken without a licensed bat ecoologist present to inpsect the feature before works commence.

This is notification of a Common Pipistrelle Maternity Roost and a Lesser Horseshoe Hibernation Roost in the three voids above Box Tunnel.  The pipistrelle bats roost in crevices in the most easterley void and the lesser Horseshoes roost in the second and third voids travelling west from the east portal.  Access to these voids should be restricted to night time surveys between April and October and should be carried out commencing 1 hour after sunset to allow bats roosting there to exit the tunnel to forage.  No crevices should be inspected unless a Licensed bat ecologist is present.  No surveys should take place between November and March so as not to distrub hibernating bats.  If inspection is urgent a licensed bat ecologist must be present.

A Great Crested Newt has been encountered at the Pixash Lane Compound on an embankment on the UP lineside approximately 8m from the track. It was an Adult Male.  The GCN was disturbed and not injured.  The newt was collected by the on-site ecologist and check for injury beofre being place in one hibernaculated located within the adjacent receptor area.  Connectivity for newts will be provided by exisitn grail line and a culvert under the access for the RRAP.  The work type was Supervision of Initial Groundworks for RRAP installation.  The contractor is Hochtief.  The watching brief was being undertaken by Sophie Carr who encountered the newt.  This incident happened on 1st November 2016 at 12pm.  GCN DNA test was completed in Apr 2016 from a nearby pond within the vicinity of the compound and it was found to be positive for Great Crested Newts.
